```
     1→%% 33-parallel3.tex — Parallel 3: The Decipherers
     2→
     3→\section{Parallel~3: Domain Ontologies and Alignment Patterns}
     4→\label{sec:parallel3}
     5→
     6→In Borges' Library, the most desperate response to meaninglessness was the
     7→emergence of the Decipherers: those who believed the Library had a hidden
     8→organisational principle, a master key that would make every book
     9→legible~\cite{borges1998library}.
    10→%
    11→Domain ontologies play that role for the DDB.
    12→%
    13→They do not impose new structure on existing records; they restore the
    14→semantic orientation that EDM's design trade-offs removed.
    15→%
    16→\textsc{mocho} (Mid-level Ontology for Cross-domain Heritage Objects) makes
    17→this operational: it aligns EDM's flat model to four GLAM-spanning domain
    18→ontologies through four patterns that can be applied to existing records
    19→without re-ingestion or schema migration.
    20→
    21→\subsection{Why Align EDM to Domain Ontologies?}
    22→\label{sec:why-align}
    23→
    24→Knowledge graph search requires explicitly-modelled semantics: typed entities,
    25→controlled relations, and event-based relationships that can be traversed rather
    26→than filtered.
    27→%
    28→EDM's design, reviewed in Section~\ref{sec:parallel1}, lacks these prerequisites.
    29→%
    30→Even a knowledge graph built directly over EDM triples is limited to traversing
    31→\texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} nodes connected by undifferentiated Dublin Core
    32→properties: there is no Work-level anchor to group related objects, no role
    33→distinction to separate creator from subject, and no event structure to express
    34→production or derivation.
    35→%
    36→Domain ontologies supply precisely these missing layers; alignment patterns make
    37→them applicable to EDM records at scale.
    38→
    39→Table~\ref{tab:edm-vs-mocho} summarises the shift across four dimensions.
    40→%
    41→The contrast is not merely terminological: entity-typed search enables queries
    42→that are structurally impossible in an EDM-only knowledge graph, as the
    43→running example in Section~\ref{sec:wemi} demonstrates.
    44→
    45→\begin{table}[tbp]
    46→  \caption{EDM only vs.\ EDM + domain ontologies across key dimensions.}
    47→  \label{tab:edm-vs-mocho}
    48→  \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
    49→  \begin{tabular}{p{2.8cm}p{3.4cm}p{4.4cm}}
    50→    \toprule
    51→    Dimension       & EDM only               & EDM + domain ontologies \\
    52→    \midrule
    53→    Search strategy & Facet filtering        & Entity + graph traversal \\
    54→    Semantics       & Literal strings,
    55→                      uncontrolled           & Typed entities,
    56→                                               controlled vocabularies \\
    57→    Relevance       & String matching        & Semantic proximity \\
    58→    Explainability  & Field-value match      & Typed relation path \\
    59→    \bottomrule
    60→  \end{tabular}
    61→\end{table}
    62→
    63→\subsection{Domain Ontologies as Decipherers}
    64→\label{sec:domain-ontos}
    65→
    66→No single domain ontology covers the full range of GLAM sectors.
    67→%
    68→\textsc{mocho} draws on four ontology families, each addressing a distinct
    69→institutional model.
    70→
    71→\textit{RDA (Resource Description and Access).}
    72→RDA is \textsc{mocho}'s primary bibliographic target.
    73→%
    74→The Deutsche Nationalbibliothek adopted RDA in 2015, and its class and property
    75→hierarchy directly represents the LRM conceptual model, making it the most
    76→practically grounded entry point for library-sector alignment~\cite{rda}.
    77→%
    78→FaBiO~\cite{peroni2012fabio} is retained in \textsc{mocho} v0.1 for backwards
    79→compatibility with the mapping exercise of Tan et al.~\cite{tan2021ddb}, but it
    80→is not the primary alignment target for new work: FRBRoo has been superseded by
    81→LRMoo~\cite{lrmoo2022}, and LRM-level concepts are now well-represented in RDA.
    82→
    83→\textit{CIDOC CRM and LRMoo.}
    84→Museum objects are naturally modelled as products of events rather than as
    85→documents: a portrait of Goethe is the outcome of a production event carried
    86→out by a named artist at a known place and time.
    87→%
    88→CIDOC CRM~\cite{cidoccrm2021} provides the event-centric machinery ---
    89→\texttt{crm:E12\_Production}, \texttt{crm:E36\_Visual\_Item} --- and LRMoo
    90→harmonises this event model with the FRBR/LRM conceptual hierarchy.
    91→%
    92→The Rijksmuseum linked data project is an instructive precedent for this
    93→alignment in practice~\cite{dijkshoorn2018rijksmuseum}.
    94→
    95→\textit{RiC-O.}
    96→Archival description follows neither the bibliographic document model nor the
    97→museum event model: its fundamental concept is the record hierarchy ---
    98→fonds, series, file, item --- along with the provenance chain that explains why
    99→records exist together~\cite{rico2021}.
   100→%
   101→Records in Context Ontology (RiC-O) formalises these archival concepts and is
   102→the alignment target for the archive sector in \textsc{mocho}.
   103→
   104→\textit{Music Ontology and ACO.}
   105→Audio and audiovisual objects require dedicated vocabulary for performances,
   106→recordings, and broadcasts.
   107→%
   108→Music Ontology~\cite{raimond2007music} extends the FRBR/WEMI model for musical
   109→works and their realisations; the Audio Commons Ontology (ACO)~\cite{aco}
   110→extends this to audiovisual material more broadly.
   111→%
   112→Together they cover the fifth Faust object --- the Jewel Song recording ---
   113→and the film still that EDM's generic \texttt{edm:type} values can only
   114→characterise as IMAGE or SOUND.
   115→
   116→\subsection{WEMI as Pivot Entities}
   117→\label{sec:wemi}
   118→
   119→The four domain ontology families share one structural commitment: the
   120→distinction between a \textit{Work} --- an intellectual creation --- and the
   121→physical or digital objects that embody it.
   122→%
   123→This commitment originates in the IFLA FRBR model~\cite{ifla1998frbr} and
   124→is expressed, in domain-specific vocabulary, across all four families
   125→\textsc{mocho} aligns.
   126→%
   127→WEMI (Work, Expression, Manifestation, Item) is therefore the natural pivot
   128→level for cross-domain queries.
   129→
   130→\texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} cannot play this role.
   131→%
   132→It conflates all four WEMI levels into a single class: a printed book edition
   133→(a Manifestation), a digitised film still (an Item), and an intellectual work
   134→are all typed identically.
   135→%
   136→This conflation means that even a fully materialised knowledge graph over EDM
   137→triples has no stable node to serve as a Work-level anchor --- no entity to
   138→which all Manifestations of the same intellectual creation can be attached.
   139→
   140→The Faust running example makes the cost concrete.
   141→%
   142→Five DDB objects --- the 1826 book edition, the portfolio of illustrations, the
   143→1893 Weimar playbill, the 1926 Murnau film still, and the Gounod Jewel Song
   144→recording --- are Manifestations or Items realising the same Work, Goethe's
   145→\textit{Faust} (GND: \texttt{4018116-6}).
   146→%
   147→All five are typed as \texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO}; they are linked to Goethe's
   148→name as a string but not to each other, and not to a shared Work entity.
   149→%
   150→A SPARQL query over \texttt{mocho:Work <gnd:4018116-6>} retrieves all five;
   151→no EDM-only query can do this, because there is no Work node to anchor the
   152→traversal.
   153→
   154→\begin{figure}[tbp]
   155→  \centering
   156→  % TODO: insert Fig 2 — five Faust objects linked to mocho:Work node
   157→  \caption{Five DDB objects related to Goethe's \textit{Faust} linked through
   158→           a shared \texttt{mocho:Work} entity (GND: \texttt{4018116-6}).
   159→           Without WEMI typing, EDM provides no node to serve as a
   160→           cross-domain anchor.}
   161→  \label{fig:faust-wemi}
   162→\end{figure}
   163→
   164→\subsection{MOCHO: A Mid-level Ontology for Cross-domain Heritage Objects}
   165→\label{sec:mocho}
   166→
   167→\paragraph{Design rationale.}
   168→\textsc{mocho} sits between EDM (flat, domain-agnostic) and the four domain
   169→ontology families (richly expressive, institutionally specific).
   170→%
   171→Its namespace is \texttt{https://ise-fizkarlsruhe.github.io/ddbkg/ddbo\#}; the
   172→current release is v0.1 (\texttt{mocho-v0.1.ttl}), which imports EDM and
   173→treats \texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} as its anchor.
   174→
   175→The key design decision --- and the central lesson from Tan et al.~\cite{tan2021ddb}
   176→--- is to type at the \textit{WEMI level} rather than at the level of individual
   177→FaBiO subclasses.
   178→%
   179→Tan et al.\ attempted to map \texttt{dc:type} values in DDB-EDM records to
   180→FaBiO subclasses, and found it impractical at scale: a single \texttt{dc:type}
   181→value can simultaneously represent document type, structural form, production
   182→process, institutional purpose, or subject matter, and the mapping requires
   183→manual curation that does not generalise across providers.
   184→%
   185→\textsc{mocho} avoids this by using \texttt{dc:type} and
   186→\texttt{ddb:hierarchyType} values only as heuristics for assigning WEMI-level
   187→types --- Work, Expression, Manifestation, Item --- without attempting to
   188→resolve the fine-grained subclass ambiguity that defeated the earlier approach.
   189→%
   190→The result is a significantly coarser but far more robust typing that is
   191→applicable to the full DDB at scale.
   192→
   193→\textsc{mocho} is designed to be reusable beyond the DDB.
   194→%
   195→Europeana, DPLA, Trove, and Gallica face the same structural problem: they
   196→aggregate heterogeneous metadata from GLAM institutions into a cross-domain
   197→container, lose domain semantics in transformation, and gain uniform access at
   198→the cost of semantic precision.
   199→%
   200→The three structural parallels and the four alignment patterns are not
   201→DDB-specific; only the heuristic \texttt{dc:type} mappings require
   202→per-aggregator calibration.
   203→
   204→\paragraph{Core WEMI classes.}
   205→\textsc{mocho} defines four WEMI classes, each as a necessary condition
   206→expressed via \texttt{owl:unionOf}:
   207→
   208→\begin{lstlisting}[language=Turtle,caption={\textsc{mocho} Work class (abbreviated).
   209→Full \texttt{owl:unionOf} members at companion website.},
   210→                   label=lst:mocho-work]
   211→mocho:Work rdfs:subClassOf [
   212→  owl:unionOf ( frbroo:F1_Work  lrmoo:F1_Work  mo:MusicalWork
   213→                fabio:Work  frbr:Work  rda:C10001
   214→                crm:E89_Propositional_Object
   215→                rico:Record  rico:RecordResource
   216→                rico:RecordSet ) ] .
   217→\end{lstlisting}
   218→
   219→The \texttt{owl:unionOf} formulation is a necessary condition: every
   220→\texttt{mocho:Work} instance must belong to at least one domain-specific Work
   221→class from the union.
   222→%
   223→This serves two functions simultaneously.
   224→%
   225→First, it \textit{validates} the typing: an instance cannot be asserted as
   226→\texttt{mocho:Work} without belonging to at least one domain ontology's Work
   227→class, preventing the class from becoming an unconstrained catch-all.
   228→%
   229→Second, it \textit{enables} cross-domain queries: an instance typed as both
   230→\texttt{mocho:Work} and \texttt{rda:C10001} participates in domain-specific
   231→SPARQL queries against RDA-aware endpoints \textit{and} in cross-domain queries
   232→against the \textsc{mocho} Work axis simultaneously --- the same node is
   233→legible in multiple hexagons.
   234→
   235→Expression, Manifestation, and Item are defined analogously.
   236→%
   237→\texttt{mocho:Expression} includes \texttt{frbroo:F2\_Expression},
   238→\texttt{lrmoo:F2\_Expression}, \texttt{mo:MusicalExpression},
   239→\texttt{rda:C10006}, \texttt{crm:E73\_Information\_Object},
   240→\texttt{ac:AudioExpression}, and archival equivalents from RiC-O.
   241→%
   242→\texttt{mocho:Manifestation} adds \texttt{frbroo:F3\_Manifestation\_Product\_Type},
   243→\texttt{lrmoo:F3\_Manifestation}, \texttt{rda:C10007},
   244→\texttt{ac:AudioManifestation}, and \texttt{rico:Instantiation}.
   245→%
   246→\texttt{mocho:Item} grounds out at the physical level via
   247→\texttt{crm:E24\_Physical\_Human-Made\_Thing}, \texttt{rda:C10003},
   248→\texttt{ac:AudioItem}, and \texttt{rico:Instantiation}.
   249→
   250→\paragraph{WEMI properties.}
   251→Four properties connect the WEMI levels:
   252→\texttt{mocho:realization}/\texttt{realizationOf} (Work\,$\to$\,Expression),
   253→\texttt{mocho:embodiment}/\texttt{embodimentOf} (Expression\,$\to$\,Manifestation),
   254→\texttt{mocho:exemplar}/\texttt{exemplarOf} (Manifestation\,$\to$\,Item), and
   255→\texttt{mocho:representation}/\texttt{representationOf} (Expression\,$\to$\,Item,
   256→a shortcut for cases where the Expression level is not separately represented
   257→in the data).
   258→%
   259→Two structural properties handle derivation:
   260→\texttt{mocho:derivativeOf} (a sub-property of \texttt{edm:isDerivativeOf})
   261→and \texttt{mocho:adaptationOf} (a sub-property of \texttt{mocho:derivativeOf}).
   262→%
   263→These are sufficient to express, for example, that Gounod's opera is an
   264→adaptation of Goethe's text, and that Murnau's film is a further derivative of
   265→both.
   266→
   267→\paragraph{Four alignment patterns.}
   268→
   269→\subparagraph{Pattern~1: Multiple Typing.}
   270→The primary mechanism for cross-domain queries is to type the same node as both
   271→\texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} and a \textsc{mocho} WEMI class.
   272→%
   273→This preserves full backwards compatibility with EDM consumers while adding the
   274→typed entity that \textsc{mocho}-aware queries require.
   275→%
   276→A link to a GND authority record via \texttt{mocho:realizationOf} then provides
   277→the Work-level anchor:
   278→
   279→\begin{lstlisting}[language=Turtle,caption={Pattern~1: Multiple Typing.
   280→The same node participates in EDM and in MOCHO-aware graph traversal.},
   281→                   label=lst:pattern1]
   282→<ddb:item/12345> a edm:ProvidedCHO , mocho:Manifestation ;
   283→    dc:title "Faust. Eine Tragedie" ;
   284→    mocho:realizationOf <gnd:4018116-6> .  # Faust (Goethe)
   285→\end{lstlisting}
   286→
   287→Pattern~1 is sufficient to enable the cross-domain Work query: any
   288→\textsc{mocho}-aware endpoint can now retrieve all five Faust objects by
   289→traversing \texttt{mocho:realizationOf} links to \texttt{<gnd:4018116-6>},
   290→regardless of the institutional sector or source schema of the originating
   291→record.
   292→
   293→\subparagraph{Pattern~2: Subproperties.}
   294→EDM's property conflation (Section~\ref{sec:parallel1}) can be partially
   295→reversed by asserting domain-specific properties as sub-properties of
   296→the generic Dublin Core terms they shadow.
   297→%
   298→This preserves backwards compatibility for consumers that query on
   299→\texttt{dc:contributor} or \texttt{dc:creator}, while making precise role
   300→assertions available to consumers that can use them:
   301→
   302→\begin{lstlisting}[language=Turtle,caption={Pattern~2: Subproperties.
   303→Domain-specific roles refine generic DC properties without breaking
   304→EDM consumers.},label=lst:pattern2]
   305→rdaa:P50450  rdfs:subPropertyOf dc:contributor .  # has editor
   306→rdaw:P10203  rdfs:subPropertyOf dc:creator .      # has screenwriter
   307→rdaa:P50190  rdfs:subPropertyOf dc:creator .      # has composer
   308→
   309→<ddb:item/12345> rdaw:P10203 <gnd:118585imdb> .  # has screenwriter
   310→\end{lstlisting}
   311→
   312→A query on \texttt{dc:creator} still returns the screenwriter triple via
   313→sub-property inference; a query on \texttt{rdaw:P10203} retrieves only the
   314→screenwriting role.
   315→%
   316→The information was present in the source schema (a MARC~700 relator term, or
   317→a LIDO event role); Pattern~2 recovers it without re-ingestion.
   318→
   319→\subparagraph{Pattern~3: Event- or Object-Centric.}
   320→Museum and archival objects require event-based modelling; bibliographic and
   321→most library objects are better served by an object-centric model.
   322→%
   323→\textsc{mocho} supports both, bridging them through \texttt{edm:Event} and
   324→the CIDOC CRM event hierarchy.
   325→
   326→The 1826 book edition is object-centric: the record describes a Manifestation
   327→with properties attached directly to the object node --- publication date,
   328→publisher, physical description.
   329→%
   330→The 1926 Murnau film still is event-centric: its description is most naturally
   331→structured as a production event (\texttt{crm:E12\_Production}) that produced
   332→a visual item (\texttt{crm:E36\_Visual\_Item}), carried out by a film crew at
   333→a particular place and time.
   334→%
   335→Goethe's illustrations portfolio occupies a middle position: the artworks are
   336→physical objects, but their attribution is an event --- a creation act ---
   337→linking artist, medium, and time.
   338→
   339→\begin{figure}[tbp]
   340→  \centering
   341→  % TODO: Fig 3 — event-centric vs. object-centric diagram
   342→  % (a) 1826 book edition: object-centric with mocho:Manifestation node
   343→  % (b) 1926 film still: event-centric via crm:E12_Production → crm:E36_Visual_Item
   344→  \caption{Pattern~3: object-centric model (1826 book edition, left) vs.\
   345→           event-centric model (1926 film still, right).
   346→           Both nodes are typed as \texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} in the DDB;
   347→           \textsc{mocho} alignment exposes the structural difference.}
   348→  \label{fig:event-vs-object}
   349→\end{figure}
   350→
   351→Pattern~3 does not require choosing one model globally.
   352→%
   353→Because both paths connect to \texttt{mocho:Manifestation} or
   354→\texttt{mocho:Item} via Pattern~1, a cross-domain query over the Work axis
   355→retrieves objects from both modelling conventions without the query author
   356→needing to know which convention applies to each record.
   357→
   358→\subparagraph{Pattern~4: Union-of Inheritance.}
   359→Patterns~1 and~4 work together to enable cross-domain traversal.
   360→%
   361→Pattern~1 types a node as \texttt{mocho:Manifestation}; Pattern~4's
   362→\texttt{owl:unionOf} necessary condition ensures that this typing is only valid
   363→if the node also belongs to at least one domain-specific Manifestation class:
   364→
   365→\begin{lstlisting}[language=Turtle,caption={Pattern~4: Union-of Inheritance
   366→validates cross-domain typing. Abbreviated; see Listing~\ref{lst:mocho-work}
   367→for Work.},label=lst:pattern4]
   368→mocho:Manifestation rdfs:subClassOf [
   369→  owl:unionOf ( frbroo:F3_Manifestation_Product_Type
   370→                lrmoo:F3_Manifestation  rda:C10007
   371→                mo:MusicalManifestation fabio:Manifestation
   372→                frbr:Manifestation  crm:E73_Information_Object
   373→                ac:AudioManifestation  rico:Instantiation ) ] .
   374→\end{lstlisting}
   375→
   376→An instance typed as \texttt{mocho:Manifestation} and \texttt{rda:C10007}
   377→satisfies the union constraint and participates in both RDA-aware and
   378→cross-domain SPARQL queries.
   379→%
   380→An instance typed only as \texttt{mocho:Manifestation} without any domain
   381→membership is flagged by a reasoner as under-specified, providing a
   382→consistency check on the alignment process itself.
   383→
   384→\subsection{Alignment Strategies for Existing EDM Records}
   385→\label{sec:alignment-strategies}
   386→
   387→The four patterns can be applied to existing DDB-EDM records without
   388→modifying the underlying records, re-running ingest pipelines, or requiring
   389→provider cooperation.
   390→%
   391→Two complementary strategies drive the enrichment.
   392→
   393→\textit{Heuristic typing.}
   394→\texttt{dc:type} and \texttt{ddb:hierarchyType} field values can be mapped
   395→to \textsc{mocho} WEMI subclasses by rule.
   396→%
   397→For example, \texttt{dc:type}\,=\,``Buch'' or
   398→\texttt{ddb:hierarchyType}\,=\,``volume'' consistently predicts a Manifestation
   399→in the library sector; \texttt{edm:type}\,=\,``IMAGE'' combined with a LIDO
   400→source schema consistently predicts a museum Item or Manifestation.
   401→%
   402→These rules are aggregator-specific in their thresholds but structurally
   403→reusable.
   404→%
   405→A full \texttt{dc:type}\,$\to$\,MOCHO mapping table is provided on the
   406→companion website.
   407→
   408→\textit{Statistical and NER-based enrichment.}
   409→Heuristic rules address modality and structural type but not entity identity.
   410→%
   411→Fine-grained named entity recognition over literal values --- titles,
   412→contributor strings, date fields --- can surface entity candidates that are
   413→then linked to authority file URIs (GND, Wikidata, Getty vocabularies) and
   414→mapped to \textsc{mocho} properties.
   415→%
   416→A contributor string ``hrsg.\ von'' identifies an editorial role suitable for
   417→\texttt{rdaa:P50450}; a place name in a \texttt{dc:spatial} field can be
   418→linked to a TGN or Wikidata URI suitable for \texttt{edm:Place}.
   419→%
   420→Where entity linking confidence is low, the enriched triples carry a
   421→provenance annotation rather than being asserted as ground truth.
   422→
   423→\medskip
   424→\noindent
   425→\textbf{Parallel~3.}
   426→Domain ontologies are the Decipherers: they restore the semantic orientation
   427→that EDM's design trade-offs removed.
   428→%
   429→\textsc{mocho} makes this operational by aligning EDM's flat model to four
   430→GLAM-spanning domain ontologies through four patterns --- Multiple Typing,
   431→Subproperties, Event- or Object-Centric, and Union-of Inheritance --- that
   432→can be applied to existing records without re-ingestion.
   433→%
   434→The Work-level anchor that emerges from this alignment is what allows five
   435→semantically connected but institutionally disparate Faust objects to be
   436→recognised as realisations of the same intellectual creation.
   437→



```

### User *(2026-02-26T09:03:06.158Z)*

**Tool result**
```
abstract = {Many applications benefit from the use of a suitable ontology but it can be difficult to determine which ontology is best suited to a particular application. Although ontology evaluation techniques are improving as more measures and methodologies are proposed, the literature contains few specific examples of cohesive evaluation activity that links ontologies, applications and their requirements, and measures and methodologies. In this paper, we present ROMEO, a requirements-oriented methodology for evaluating ontologies, and apply it to the task of evaluating the suitability of some general ontologies (variants of sub-domains of the Wikipedia category structure) for supporting browsing in Wikipedia. The ROMEO methodology identifies requirements that an ontology must satisfy, and maps these requirements to evaluation measures. We validate part of this mapping with a task-based evaluation method involving users, and report on our findings from this user study.}
```

### Assistant *(2026-02-26T09:04:03.060Z)*

**Tool call:** `Write`
```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/mocho/docs/ontology-mapping-algorithm.md",
  "content": "# Ontology Mapping Algorithm\n\n## Overview\n\nThe domain-ontology-to-RDA mappings used in MOCHO are produced by two scripts\nwith complementary roles:\n\n- `scripts/gen_frbr_target_mapping.py` — automated BFS traversal over a target\n  ontology graph; covers WEMI classes and properties with declared FRBR ancestry\n- `scripts/gen_nonwemi_mapping.py` — hand-curated lookup table for FRBR Group 2\n  (agents), Group 3 (subjects), and FaBiO utility terms that lack the structural\n  regularity needed for automated traversal\n\nBoth produce the same output schema (`source, rda, domain, range, match, remarks`)\nso automated and manual mappings are interchangeable downstream.\n\n---\n\n## Automated traversal (`gen_frbr_target_mapping.py`)\n\n### Inputs\n\n- The target ontology in any rdflib-supported format (N3, Turtle, RDF/XML, …)\n- Two base mapping CSVs:\n  - `frbr-fabio-to-rda.csv` — FRBR WEMI classes and properties → RDA\n  - `frbr-fabio-nonwemi-to-rda.csv` — FRBR Group 2/3 and FaBiO → RDA\n\n### Class mapping\n\n1. For each `owl:Class` in the target namespace, run BFS upward through\n   `rdfs:subClassOf` edges until a **FRBR anchor IRI** is found or the graph is\n   exhausted. Anchor IRIs are hardcoded: all FRBR Group 1–3 classes, their\n   `foaf:` proxies (Agent, Person, Group, Organization), and `event:Event`.\n2. The anchor determines the **FRBR group** (G1 = WEMI, G2 = agents, G3 =\n   subjects, Event = event.owl).\n3. The group + BFS depth determine **match quality**:\n   - **direct** — one-hop to a G1 anchor, and that anchor's FRBR→RDA mapping is\n     itself direct; no foaf proxy involved\n   - **approximate** — any of: depth > 1 (criterion E), foaf proxy used (B),\n     or the FRBR anchor's own RDA mapping is approximate (inherited)\n   - **no match** — no FRBR anchor reachable in the class hierarchy\n\nFor cross-standard alignment classes (e.g. `rico:RecordResource`, which spans\n`frbr:Work` and `frbr:Expression`), a self-check is performed before BFS: if\nthe class URI itself is in the anchor table, it is recognised directly without\ntraversal, and receives a compound RDA value\n(e.g. `rdac:C10001 \"Work\"; rdac:C10006 \"Expression\"`).\n\n### Property mapping\n\nTwo strategies are tried in order:\n\n**Strategy 1 — subPropertyOf chain.** BFS upward through `rdfs:subPropertyOf`\n(and `owl:equivalentProperty`) edges until a hardcoded FRBR property anchor is\nfound (e.g. `frbr:exemplar`, `frbr:realization`, `frbr:creator`). The RDA\nmapping for that anchor is looked up in the base CSV and inherited.\n\n**Strategy 2 — domain/range pattern inference.** If no FRBR super-property is\nfound, the declared `rdfs:domain` and `rdfs:range` are each resolved to their\nFRBR group via the same BFS used for classes. The group-pair pattern determines\nthe closest FRBR bridge property:\n\n| Domain group | Range group | Inferred FRBR bridge |\n|---|---|---|\n| G1 | G2 | `frbr:creator` / `frbr:realizer` / `frbr:producer` / `frbr:owner` (by WEMI level) |\n| G2 | G1 | Inverse agent-role |\n| G1 | G1 | `frbr:part` (structural / part-whole) |\n| G1 | G3 | `frbr:subject` |\n| Event | G1 | `frbr:realization` (criterion F: event mediation) |\n| G1 | Event | `frbr:realization` (criterion F) |\n\nAll domain/range inferences are **approximate** by construction.\n\n### Match quality criterion codes\n\n| Code | Name | Trigger |\n|---|---|---|\n| A | Granularity split | Source maps to multiple role-specific RDA properties |\n| B | Semantic narrowing | foaf proxy or weaker RDA equivalent used |\n| E | Transitive subtype | Anchor reached via 2+ hops; no specific RDA sub-class |\n| F | Event mediation | Ontology routes agent/WEMI link through an event class |\n| B (self) | Cross-standard alignment | Class is itself an explicit anchor spanning multiple FRBR levels |\n\n### What \"direct\" means\n\n\"Direct\" is a formal graph property, not a lexical or semantic judgment. A\nmapping is direct if and only if:\n1. The ontology file contains a `rdfs:subClassOf` or `rdfs:subPropertyOf` triple\n   linking the source term to a FRBR anchor at depth 1.\n2. That anchor's own FRBR→RDA mapping is direct in the base CSV.\n\nNo string comparison, label matching, or synonym detection is performed anywhere\nin the algorithm.\n\n### Outputs\n\n- `output/<prefix>-to-rda.csv` and `.json`\n- `mocho-odk/imports/<prefix>_terms.txt` — IRIs of all direct and approximate\n  source terms, for ODK import\n\n---\n\n## Manual lookup table (`gen_nonwemi_mapping.py`)\n\nCovers FRBR Group 2 (responsible entities), Group 3 (subjects), and FaBiO\nutility properties. These require judgment rather than traversal:\n\n- `frbr:producer` maps to three distinct RDA roles (producer, manufacturer,\n  publisher) — no algorithm could enumerate the right set of RDA properties from\n  structural subsumption alone\n- `frbr:subject` fans out to 42 type-specific RDA subject properties\n- `frbr:Concept` has no RDA equivalent class; RDA externalises concepts to SKOS\n\nThe script is a serializer: it dumps a static Python list to CSV and JSON,\napplying the same match criteria and output schema as the automated tool.\n"
}
```

  "old_string": "\\subsection{MOCHO: A Mid-level Ontology for Cross-domain Heritage Objects}\n\\label{sec:mocho}",
  "new_string": "\\subsection{Formalising WEMI Alignments: A Structural Subsumption Method}\n\\label{sec:alignment-method}\n\nAsserting that \\texttt{rico:RecordResource} belongs to \\texttt{mocho:Work}\nrequires a principled basis.\n%\n\\textsc{mocho} grounds these assertions in a reproducible, ontology-level\nmethod: breadth-first traversal of \\texttt{rdfs:subClassOf} and\n\\texttt{rdfs:subPropertyOf} chains in each domain ontology, halting at a fixed\nset of FRBR anchor IRIs~\\cite{ifla1998frbr}.\n\n\\paragraph{Class alignment.}\nFor each class in a target namespace, the algorithm searches upward through\nthe subclass graph.\n%\nIf a FRBR Group~1 anchor (\\texttt{frbr:Work}, \\texttt{frbr:Expression},\n\\texttt{frbr:Manifestation}, \\texttt{frbr:Item}) is reachable in one hop and\nthat anchor's FRBR\\,$\\to$\\,RDA\\,\\cite{rda} mapping is itself direct, the class\nreceives a \\textit{direct} match.\n%\nA path of two or more hops, or one mediated by a \\texttt{foaf:Agent} proxy,\nyields an \\textit{approximate} match with a diagnostic criterion code: \\textbf{E}\n(transitive subtype, no specific RDA sub-class) or \\textbf{B} (semantic\nnarrowing via proxy).\n%\nClasses with no reachable FRBR anchor receive \\textit{no match}.\n\nThe criterion is formal, not lexical: the algorithm reads only\n\\texttt{rdfs:subClassOf} triples in the loaded graph.\n%\nNo label comparison, embedding distance, or synonym lookup is performed.\n%\nThis also applies to cross-standard anchors: \\texttt{rico:RecordResource}\nspans both \\texttt{frbr:Work} and \\texttt{frbr:Expression}\n(Section~\\ref{sec:domain-ontos}), and is registered directly in the anchor\ntable rather than reached by traversal, yielding a compound RDA target\n(\\texttt{rdac:C10001}; \\texttt{rdac:C10006}) and criterion \\textbf{B} (cross-standard\nalignment).\n\n\\paragraph{Property alignment.}\nProperties are handled by two strategies applied in order.\n%\n\\textit{Structural:} BFS over \\texttt{rdfs:subPropertyOf} edges to a hardcoded\nFRBR property anchor (\\texttt{frbr:exemplar}, \\texttt{frbr:realization},\n\\texttt{frbr:creator}, etc.); the RDA mapping is inherited from the base table.\n%\n\\textit{Domain/range inference:} if no FRBR super-property is found, the declared\ndomain and range are each resolved to their FRBR group via the class traversal\nabove, and the group-pair pattern selects the closest FRBR bridge property ---\nG1\\,$\\to$\\,G2 implies an agent-role property; G1\\,$\\to$\\,G1 implies part-whole;\nEvent\\,$\\to$\\,G1 implies realisation (criterion \\textbf{F}: the ontology routes\nan agent/WEMI link through an event class where FRBR uses a direct property).\n%\nAll domain/range inferences are approximate by construction.\n\n\\paragraph{Non-WEMI terms.}\nFRBR Group~2 (responsible entities), Group~3 (subjects), and utility\nproperties from FaBiO~\\cite{peroni2012fabio} cannot be handled by structural\ntraversal, because they lack the one-to-one FRBR\\,$\\to$\\,RDA correspondence\nthat makes traversal reliable for WEMI terms.\n%\nThese are encoded in a hand-curated table using the same output schema, keeping\nautomated and manual mappings interchangeable at the point of use.\n%\nAll mapping data and the traversal scripts are provided in the companion\nrepository.\n\n\\subsection{MOCHO: A Mid-level Ontology for Cross-domain Heritage Objects}\n\\label{sec:mocho}"
}
```

   335→
   336→\subparagraph{Pattern~1: Multiple Typing.}
   337→The primary mechanism for cross-domain queries is to type the same node as both
   338→\texttt{edm:ProvidedCHO} and a \textsc{mocho} WEMI class.
   339→%
   340→This preserves full backwards compatibility with EDM consumers while adding the
   341→typed entity that \textsc{mocho}-aware queries require.

   164→\subsection{Formalising WEMI Alignments: A Structural Subsumption Method}
   165→\label{sec:alignment-method}
   166→
   167→Asserting that \texttt{rico:RecordResource} belongs to \texttt{mocho:Work}
   168→requires a principled basis.
   169→%
   170→\textsc{mocho} grounds these assertions in a reproducible, ontology-level
   171→method: breadth-first traversal of \texttt{rdfs:subClassOf} and
   172→\texttt{rdfs:subPropertyOf} chains in each domain ontology, halting at a fixed
   173→set of FRBR anchor IRIs~\cite{ifla1998frbr}.
   174→
   175→\noindent\textit{Class alignment.}
   176→For each class in a target namespace, the algorithm searches upward through
   177→the subclass graph.
   178→%
   179→If a FRBR Group~1 anchor (\texttt{frbr:Work}, \texttt{frbr:Expression},
   180→\texttt{frbr:Manifestation}, \texttt{frbr:Item}) is reachable in one hop and
   181→that anchor's FRBR\,$\to$\,RDA~\cite{rda} mapping is itself direct, the class
   182→receives a \textit{direct} match.
   183→%
   184→A path of two or more hops, or one mediated by a \texttt{foaf:Agent} proxy,
   185→yields an \textit{approximate} match with a diagnostic criterion code: \textbf{E}
   186→(transitive subtype, no specific RDA sub-class) or \textbf{B} (semantic
   187→narrowing via proxy).
   188→%
   189→Classes with no reachable FRBR anchor receive \textit{no match}.
   190→The criterion is formal, not lexical: the algorithm reads only
   191→\texttt{rdfs:subClassOf} triples in the loaded graph; no label comparison,
   192→embedding distance, or synonym lookup is performed.
   193→%
   194→Cross-standard anchors such as \texttt{rico:RecordResource} --- which spans
   195→both \texttt{frbr:Work} and \texttt{frbr:Expression}
   196→(Section~\ref{sec:domain-ontos}) --- are registered directly in the anchor
   197→table, yielding a compound RDA target (\texttt{rdac:C10001}; \texttt{rdac:C10006})
   198→with criterion \textbf{B}.
   199→
   200→\noindent\textit{Property alignment.}
   201→Properties are handled by two strategies in order.
   202→%
   203→\textit{Structural}: BFS over \texttt{rdfs:subPropertyOf} edges to a hardcoded
   204→FRBR property anchor (\texttt{frbr:exemplar}, \texttt{frbr:realization},
   205→\texttt{frbr:creator}, etc.); the RDA mapping is inherited from the base table.
   206→%
   207→\textit{Domain/range inference}: if no FRBR super-property is found, the declared
   208→domain and range are each resolved to their FRBR group via the class traversal
   209→above, and the group-pair pattern selects the closest FRBR bridge property ---
   210→G1\,$\to$\,G2 implies an agent-role property; G1\,$\to$\,G1 implies part-whole;
   211→Event\,$\to$\,G1 implies realisation (criterion \textbf{F}: the ontology routes
   212→an agent/WEMI link through an event class where FRBR uses a direct property).
   213→All domain/range inferences are approximate by construction.
   214→
   215→\noindent\textit{Non-WEMI terms.}
   216→FRBR Group~2 (responsible entities), Group~3 (subjects), and utility
   217→properties from FaBiO~\cite{peroni2012fabio} cannot be handled by structural
   218→traversal, because they lack the one-to-one FRBR\,$\to$\,RDA correspondence
   219→that makes traversal reliable for WEMI terms.
   220→%
   221→These are encoded in a hand-curated table using the same output schema, keeping
   222→automated and manual mappings interchangeable at the point of use.
   223→%
   224→All mapping data and traversal scripts are provided in the companion repository.
   225→
   226→\subsection{MOCHO: A Mid-level Ontology for Cross-domain Heritage Objects}
   227→\label{sec:mocho}
   228→
   229→\paragraph{Design rationale.}
   230→\textsc{mocho} sits between EDM (flat, domain-agnostic) and the four domain
   231→ontology families (richly expressive, institutionally specific).
   232→%
   233→Its namespace is \texttt{https://ise-fizkarlsruhe.github.io/ddbkg/ddbo\#}; the

### Assistant *(2026-02-26T09:35:17.334Z)*

Looks clean. The fix: replaced the three `\paragraph{...}` commands with `\noindent\textit{...}` inline heading style. This matches how the document patterns sub-items elsewhere (e.g. `\textit{Heuristic typing.}` / `\textit{Statistical and NER-based enrichment.}` in §6.5) and adds zero sectioning groups to TeX's stack.

The root cause was cumulative: `\paragraph` in LaTeX opens a group that stays open until the next sectioning command. The `listings` package also pushes many groups for each `lstlisting` block. The three additional `\paragraph` commands I added were enough to push the total past 255 when the later `lstlisting` blocks in the MOCHO section ran.

### User *(2026-03-03T18:35:06.597Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→"""
     2→extract_rda_classes.py
     3→
     4→Purpose:
     5→    Parse RDA vocabulary XML files (from the RDA-Vocabularies release) and
     6→    extract all OWL class declarations. Outputs a JSON array and a CSV table,
     7→    each listing every class with its element ID, URI, English label, definition,
     8→    superclass chain, registry status, and source vocabulary version.
     9→
    10→    Class declarations live in `c.xml` inside each Elements directory (14
    11→    classes as of RDA Vocabularies 5.4.9). All other entity files (w.xml,
    12→    e.xml, m.xml, i.xml, a.xml, etc.) contain only property declarations.
    13→
    14→    Label extraction: the file provides both `rdfs:label` and
    15→    `rdakit:toolkitLabel`. Some `rdfs:label` values carry a spurious "rda:"
    16→    prefix (e.g. "rda:Agent"). The toolkit label is preferred; `rdfs:label`
    17→    is the fallback.
    18→
    19→    Registry status URIs are mapped to human-readable strings:
    20→        1001 → Published
    21→        1002 → Incomplete
    22→        1003 → Published of record
    23→        1008 → Deprecated
    24→
    25→Usage:
    26→    python scripts/extract_rda_classes.py [OPTIONS]
    27→
    28→    Run from the mocho/ project root.
    29→
    30→    --vocab-dir PATH|all
    31→        PATH: directory containing c.xml (the RDA Elements directory).
    32→              Default: ontology/RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9/xml/Elements
    33→        all:  recursively scan --vocab-base for all c.xml files under any
    34→              RDA-Vocabularies-* subtree and aggregate classes from all
    35→              versions found.
    36→    --vocab-base PATH
    37→        Base directory for the 'all' scan.
    38→        Default: ontology/
    39→    --output PREFIX
    40→        Prefix for output files. Writes PREFIX.json and PREFIX.csv.
    41→        Default: output/mapping_rda_classes
    42→    --tree
    43→        Print the class hierarchy as an indented tree to stdout.
    44→
    45→Inputs:
    46→    <vocab-dir>/c.xml   RDA Classes OWL/RDF file (owl:Class declarations)
    47→
    48→Outputs:
    49→    PREFIX.json   JSON array of class objects, one per class.
    50→    PREFIX.csv    CSV table with header row; one data row per class.
    51→    stdout        Summary of class counts. Hierarchy tree if --tree is set.
    52→
    53→    JSON / CSV fields per class:
    54→        element_id   rdac:C10001
    55→        uri          http://rdaregistry.info/Elements/c/C10001
    56→        label        work
    57→        subclass_of  rdac:C10013  (pipe-separated list in CSV; list in JSON)
    58→        status       Published
    59→        definition   A distinct intellectual or artistic creation …
    60→        vocab        RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9  (source release directory name)
    61→
    62→Dependencies:
    63→    Python standard library only (xml.etree.ElementTree, argparse, pathlib,
    64→    json, csv).
    65→
    66→Assumptions:
    67→    XML files conform to the RDA Vocabularies OWL/RDF schema used in
    68→    releases 5.x. English-language annotations (xml:lang="en") are expected.
    69→    Class URIs follow the pattern http://rdaregistry.info/Elements/c/C10xxx.
    70→"""
    71→
    72→import argparse
    73→import csv
    74→import json
    75→import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
    76→from pathlib import Path
    77→from typing import Optional
    78→
    79→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    80→# Namespace constants
    81→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    82→RDF    = 'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#'
    83→RDFS   = 'http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#'
    84→SKOS   = 'http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#'
    85→OWL    = 'http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#'
    86→XML_NS = 'http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace'
    87→REG    = 'http://metadataregistry.org/uri/profile/regap/'
    88→RDAKIT = 'http://metadataregistry.org/uri/profile/rdakit/'
    89→
    90→RDF_ABOUT       = f'{{{RDF}}}about'
    91→RDF_RESOURCE    = f'{{{RDF}}}resource'
    92→OWL_CLASS       = f'{{{OWL}}}Class'
    93→RDFS_SUBCLASSOF = f'{{{RDFS}}}subClassOf'
    94→RDFS_LABEL      = f'{{{RDFS}}}label'
    95→SKOS_DEF        = f'{{{SKOS}}}definition'
    96→XML_LANG        = f'{{{XML_NS}}}lang'
    97→REG_STATUS      = f'{{{REG}}}status'
    98→RDAKIT_LABEL    = f'{{{RDAKIT}}}toolkitLabel'
    99→RDAKIT_DEF      = f'{{{RDAKIT}}}toolkitDefinition'
   100→
   101→# Registry status URI suffix → human-readable string
   102→STATUS_MAP = {
   103→    '1001': 'Published',
   104→    '1002': 'Incomplete',
   105→    '1003': 'Published of record',
   106→    '1008': 'Deprecated',
   107→}
   108→
   109→RDA_CLASS_NS     = 'http://rdaregistry.info/Elements/c/'
   110→RDA_CLASS_PREFIX = 'rdac'
   111→
   112→CSV_FIELDS = ['element_id', 'uri', 'label', 'subclass_of', 'status',
   113→              'definition', 'vocab']
   114→
   115→
   116→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   117→# Helpers
   118→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   119→
   120→def _uri_to_local(uri: str) -> str:
   121→    """Return the local name from a full URI (last path segment)."""
   122→    return uri.rstrip('/').split('/')[-1]
   123→
   124→
   125→def _en_text(element, tag: str) -> str:
   126→    """Return the text of the first child <tag xml:lang='en'>, or ''."""
   127→    for child in element.findall(tag):
   128→        if child.get(XML_LANG) == 'en':
   129→            return (child.text or '').strip()
   130→    return ''
   131→
   132→
   133→def _format_id(local_id: str) -> str:
   134→    """Return a prefixed class ID, e.g. 'rdac:C10001'."""
   135→    return f'{RDA_CLASS_PREFIX}:{local_id}'
   136→
   137→
   138→def _status_label(uri: str) -> str:
   139→    """Map a reg:status URI to a human-readable string."""
   140→    suffix = _uri_to_local(uri)
   141→    return STATUS_MAP.get(suffix, f'Unknown ({suffix})')
   142→
   143→
   144→def _vocab_name(xml_path: Path) -> str:
   145→    """
   146→    Derive a vocabulary version label from an XML file path.
   147→    Looks for a 'RDA-Vocabularies-*' ancestor directory name; falls back
   148→    to the immediate parent directory name.
   149→    """
   150→    for part in reversed(xml_path.parts):
   151→        if part.startswith('RDA-Vocabularies'):
   152→            return part
   153→    return xml_path.parent.name
   154→
   155→
   156→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   157→# Parsing
   158→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   159→
   160→def parse_classes(xml_path: Path, vocab: str) -> list:
   161→    """
   162→    Parse a c.xml file and return a list of class dicts with keys:
   163→        element_id, uri, local_id, label, definition,
   164→        subclass_of (list of prefixed IDs), status, vocab
   165→    Only owl:Class elements whose URI starts with RDA_CLASS_NS are included.
   166→    """
   167→    tree = ET.parse(xml_path)
   168→    root = tree.getroot()
   169→
   170→    classes = []
   171→    for child in root:
   172→        if child.tag != OWL_CLASS:
   173→            continue
   174→        uri = child.get(RDF_ABOUT, '')
   175→        if not uri.startswith(RDA_CLASS_NS):
   176→            continue
   177→
   178→        local_id   = _uri_to_local(uri)
   179→        element_id = _format_id(local_id)
   180→
   181→        # Label: prefer rdakit:toolkitLabel; fall back to rdfs:label
   182→        label = _en_text(child, RDAKIT_LABEL) or _en_text(child, RDFS_LABEL)
   183→        # Strip spurious "rda:" prefix present on some rdfs:label values
   184→        if label.startswith('rda:'):
   185→            label = label[4:]
   186→
   187→        # Definition: prefer skos:definition; fall back to rdakit:toolkitDefinition
   188→        definition = _en_text(child, SKOS_DEF) or _en_text(child, RDAKIT_DEF)
   189→
   190→        # Superclasses within the rdac: namespace only
   191→        subclass_of = []
   192→        for sc in child.findall(RDFS_SUBCLASSOF):
   193→            parent_uri = sc.get(RDF_RESOURCE, '')
   194→            if parent_uri.startswith(RDA_CLASS_NS):
   195→                subclass_of.append(_format_id(_uri_to_local(parent_uri)))
   196→
   197→        # Registry status
   198→        status_el = child.find(REG_STATUS)
   199→        status = (
   200→            _status_label(status_el.get(RDF_RESOURCE, ''))
   201→            if status_el is not None else 'Unknown'
   202→        )
   203→
   204→        classes.append({
   205→            'element_id':  element_id,
   206→            'uri':         uri,
   207→            'local_id':    local_id,
   208→            'label':       label,
   209→            'definition':  definition,
   210→            'subclass_of': subclass_of,
   211→            'status':      status,
   212→            'vocab':       vocab,
   213→        })
   214→
   215→    # Stable sort by numeric part of local ID
   216→    classes.sort(
   217→        key=lambda c: int(c['local_id'][1:]) if c['local_id'][1:].isdigit() else 0
   218→    )
   219→    return classes
   220→
   221→
   222→def find_class_files(vocab_base: Path) -> list:
   223→    """
   224→    Recursively search vocab_base for all c.xml files that contain at least
   225→    one owl:Class declaration. Returns a list of Path objects.
   226→    """
   227→    found = []
   228→    for xml_file in sorted(vocab_base.rglob('c.xml')):
   229→        try:
   230→            tree = ET.parse(xml_file)
   231→            root = tree.getroot()
   232→            if any(child.tag == OWL_CLASS for child in root):
   233→                found.append(xml_file)
   234→        except ET.ParseError:
   235→            pass  # skip malformed XML
   236→    return found
   237→
   238→
   239→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   240→# Output writers
   241→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   242→
   243→def write_json(classes: list, path: Path) -> None:
   244→    """Write classes as a JSON array. Omits the internal 'local_id' field."""
   245→    records = [
   246→        {k: v for k, v in c.items() if k != 'local_id'}
   247→        for c in classes
   248→    ]
   249→    path.write_text(json.dumps(records, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False), encoding='utf-8')
   250→
   251→
   252→def write_csv(classes: list, path: Path) -> None:
   253→    """Write classes as a CSV table with a header row."""
   254→    with open(path, 'w', newline='', encoding='utf-8') as f:
   255→        writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=CSV_FIELDS, extrasaction='ignore')
   256→        writer.writeheader()
   257→        for c in classes:
   258→            row = dict(c)
   259→            row['subclass_of'] = ' | '.join(c['subclass_of'])
   260→            writer.writerow(row)
   261→
   262→
   263→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   264→# Tree display
   265→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   266→
   267→def print_tree(classes: list) -> None:
   268→    """Print the class hierarchy as an indented tree to stdout."""
   269→    all_ids = {c['local_id'] for c in classes}
   270→    children: dict = {}
   271→
   272→    for c in classes:
   273→        # subclass_of entries are prefixed IDs like 'rdac:C10013';
   274→        # extract the local part after ':' to match all_ids keys
   275→        parent_local = None
   276→        for pid in c['subclass_of']:
   277→            local = pid.split(':')[-1]
   278→            if local in all_ids:
   279→                parent_local = local
   280→                break
   281→        children.setdefault(parent_local, []).append(c)
   282→
   283→    def render(local_id: Optional[str], depth: int) -> None:
   284→        for c in children.get(local_id, []):
   285→            indent    = '  ' * depth
   286→            connector = '└─ ' if depth > 0 else ''
   287→            print(f"{indent}{connector}{c['element_id']}  {c['label']}")
   288→            render(c['local_id'], depth + 1)
   289→
   290→    print()
   291→    print('Class Hierarchy')
   292→    print('=' * 40)
   293→    render(None, 0)
   294→    print()
   295→
   296→
   297→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   298→# Main
   299→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   300→
   301→def main() -> None:
   302→    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
   303→        description=(
   304→            'Extract RDA class declarations from RDA Vocabularies XML files. '
   305→            'Outputs PREFIX.json and PREFIX.csv.'
   306→        )
   307→    )
   308→    parser.add_argument(
   309→        '--vocab-dir',
   310→        default='ontology/RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9/xml/Elements',
   311→        metavar='PATH|all',
   312→        help=(
   313→            'Elements directory containing c.xml, or "all" to recursively '
   314→            'scan --vocab-base for all RDA class files. '
   315→            '(default: %(default)s)'
   316→        ),
   317→    )
   318→    parser.add_argument(
   319→        '--vocab-base',
   320→        default='ontology',
   321→        metavar='PATH',
   322→        help=(
   323→            'Base directory for the "all" scan. '
   324→            'Default: %(default)s'
   325→        ),
   326→    )
   327→    parser.add_argument(
   328→        '--output', '-o',
   329→        default='output/mapping_rda_classes',
   330→        metavar='PREFIX',
   331→        help=(
   332→            'Output file prefix. Writes PREFIX.json and PREFIX.csv. '
   333→            '(default: %(default)s)'
   334→        ),
   335→    )
   336→    parser.add_argument(
   337→        '--tree',
   338→        action='store_true',
   339→        help='Print the class hierarchy as an indented tree to stdout.',
   340→    )
   341→    args = parser.parse_args()
   342→
   343→    # Collect XML files to parse
   344→    if args.vocab_dir == 'all':
   345→        vocab_base = Path(args.vocab_base)
   346→        if not vocab_base.is_dir():
   347→            raise FileNotFoundError(f'--vocab-base not found: {vocab_base}')
   348→        xml_files = find_class_files(vocab_base)
   349→        if not xml_files:
   350→            raise FileNotFoundError(
   351→                f'No c.xml files with owl:Class found under: {vocab_base}'
   352→            )
   353→    else:
   354→        elements_dir = Path(args.vocab_dir)
   355→        c_xml = elements_dir / 'c.xml'
   356→        if not c_xml.exists():
   357→            raise FileNotFoundError(f'RDA classes file not found: {c_xml}')
   358→        xml_files = [c_xml]
   359→
   360→    # Parse all found files
   361→    all_classes = []
   362→    for xml_path in xml_files:
   363→        vocab = _vocab_name(xml_path)
   364→        classes = parse_classes(xml_path, vocab)
   365→        all_classes.extend(classes)
   366→        print(f'  Parsed {len(classes):3d} classes from {xml_path}')
   367→
   368→    # Write outputs
   369→    out_prefix = Path(args.output)
   370→    out_prefix.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
   371→
   372→    json_path = out_prefix.with_suffix('.json')
   373→    csv_path  = out_prefix.with_suffix('.csv')
   374→
   375→    write_json(all_classes, json_path)
   376→    write_csv(all_classes, csv_path)
   377→
   378→    print(f'  JSON → {json_path}')
   379→    print(f'  CSV  → {csv_path}')
   380→
   381→    # Summary
   382→    by_status: dict = {}
   383→    for c in all_classes:
   384→        by_status[c['status']] = by_status.get(c['status'], 0) + 1
   385→    print(f'  Total : {len(all_classes)}')
   386→    for status, count in sorted(by_status.items()):
   387→        print(f'    {status}: {count}')
   388→
   389→    if args.tree:
   390→        print_tree(all_classes)
   391→
   392→
   393→if __name__ == '__main__':
   394→    main()
   395→



```

{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/mocho/scripts/extract_ontology_terms.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"\nextract_ontology_terms.py\n\nLoad any ontology file (Turtle, RDF/XML, OWL, N3, N-Triples, JSON-LD, …),\nextract all declared classes and properties, and write a CSV and JSON table.\n\nColumns:\n    type        — term kind: class | object property | datatype property |\n                  annotation property | property\n    label       — rdfs:label (English preferred; any language as fallback;\n                  local name from IRI if absent)\n    iri         — full IRI\n    definition  — skos:definition if present; rdfs:comment otherwise; empty if neither\n\nUsage:\n    python scripts/extract_ontology_terms.py --ontology PATH [--output PREFIX]\n\n    Run from the mocho/ project root.\n\nInputs:\n    Any rdflib-supported ontology file.\n\nOutputs:\n    PREFIX.csv   CSV table, one row per term, sorted by type then IRI.\n    PREFIX.json  JSON array, one object per term.\n\nDependencies:\n    rdflib  (pip install rdflib)\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport argparse\nimport csv\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\ntry:\n    from rdflib import Graph, URIRef, RDF, RDFS, OWL\n    from rdflib.namespace import SKOS\nexcept ImportError:\n    raise SystemExit(\"rdflib is required: pip install rdflib\")\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Term type table: (rdf:type IRI, output label, sort priority)\n# Priority determines which label wins when a term has multiple type assertions.\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\nTERM_TYPES: list[tuple[URIRef, str, int]] = [\n    (OWL.Class,               \"class\",                0),\n    (RDFS.Class,              \"class\",                0),\n    (OWL.ObjectProperty,      \"object property\",      1),\n    (OWL.DatatypeProperty,    \"datatype property\",     2),\n    (OWL.AnnotationProperty,  \"annotation property\",   3),\n    (RDF.Property,            \"property\",              4),\n]\n\nFIELDNAMES = [\"type\", \"label\", \"iri\", \"definition\"]\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Helpers\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef _best_literal(uri: URIRef, g: Graph, *predicates: URIRef) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    For each predicate in order, return the first matching literal, preferring\n    English (lang='en' or lang='en-*'), then no-lang, then any language.\n    Returns '' if nothing is found across all predicates.\n    \"\"\"\n    for pred in predicates:\n        literals = list(g.objects(uri, pred))\n        if not literals:\n            continue\n        # Prefer English\n        for lit in literals:\n            lang = getattr(lit, \"language\", None)\n            if lang and lang.lower().startswith(\"en\"):\n                return str(lit).strip()\n        # Then no language tag\n        for lit in literals:\n            if not getattr(lit, \"language\", None):\n                return str(lit).strip()\n        # Then anything\n        return str(literals[0]).strip()\n    return \"\"\n\n\ndef _local_name(iri: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Extract the local name from a full IRI (fragment or last path segment).\"\"\"\n    if \"#\" in iri:\n        return iri.rsplit(\"#\", 1)[-1]\n    return iri.rsplit(\"/\", 1)[-1]\n\n\ndef extract_terms(g: Graph) -> list[dict]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Return one dict per declared class or property in the graph.\n    Only URIRef subjects are included (blank-node class expressions are skipped).\n    When a term has multiple type assertions, the highest-priority type wins.\n    \"\"\"\n    # Collect best type for each URIRef subject\n    best: dict[URIRef, tuple[str, int]] = {}\n    for rdf_type, label, priority in TERM_TYPES:\n        for subj in g.subjects(RDF.type, rdf_type):\n            if not isinstance(subj, URIRef):\n                continue\n            current = best.get(subj)\n            if current is None or priority < current[1]:\n                best[subj] = (label, priority)\n\n    rows = []\n    for uri, (type_label, _) in best.items():\n        iri = str(uri)\n        label = _best_literal(uri, g, RDFS.label) or _local_name(iri)\n        definition = _best_literal(uri, g, SKOS.definition, RDFS.comment)\n        rows.append({\n            \"type\":       type_label,\n            \"label\":      label,\n            \"iri\":        iri,\n            \"definition\": definition,\n        })\n\n    # Sort: type priority order, then IRI alphabetically\n    type_order = {label: pri for _, label, pri in TERM_TYPES}\n    rows.sort(key=lambda r: (type_order.get(r[\"type\"], 99), r[\"iri\"]))\n    return rows\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Output writers\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef write_csv(rows: list[dict], path: Path) -> None:\n    with open(path, \"w\", newline=\"\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\n        writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=FIELDNAMES)\n        writer.writeheader()\n        writer.writerows(rows)\n\n\ndef write_json(rows: list[dict], path: Path) -> None:\n    path.write_text(json.dumps(rows, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False), encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Main\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef main() -> None:\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)\n    parser.add_argument(\n        \"--ontology\", \"-i\",\n        required=True,\n        metavar=\"PATH\",\n        help=\"Path to the ontology file (any rdflib-supported format).\",\n    )\n    parser.add_argument(\n        \"--output\", \"-o\",\n        default=None,\n        metavar=\"PREFIX\",\n        help=(\n            \"Output file prefix. Writes PREFIX.csv and PREFIX.json. \"\n            \"Default: output/<stem> where <stem> is the ontology filename without extension.\"\n        ),\n    )\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n\n    onto_path = Path(args.ontology)\n    if not onto_path.exists():\n        raise FileNotFoundError(f\"Ontology file not found: {onto_path}\")\n\n    out_prefix = Path(args.output) if args.output else Path(\"output\") / onto_path.stem\n\n    g = Graph()\n    g.parse(str(onto_path))\n    print(f\"Loaded {len(g)} triples from {onto_path}\")\n\n    rows = extract_terms(g)\n\n    out_prefix.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    csv_path  = out_prefix.with_suffix(\".csv\")\n    json_path = out_prefix.with_suffix(\".json\")\n    write_csv(rows, csv_path)\n    write_json(rows, json_path)\n\n    print(f\"Wrote {len(rows)} terms → {csv_path}\")\n    print(f\"Wrote {len(rows)} terms → {json_path}\")\n\n    # Summary by type\n    from collections import Counter\n    counts = Counter(r[\"type\"] for r in rows)\n    for type_label, n in sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):\n        print(f\"  {type_label}: {n}\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"
}
```

     1→# SCRIPTS.md

     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## scripts/gen_mocho_diagram.py
     8→
     9→**Purpose**: Generate WEMI (Work, Expression, Manifestation, Item) superclass hierarchy diagrams for the mocho ontology, showing `rdfs:subClassOf`, `owl:unionOf`, and `owl:equivalentClass` relationships across aligned ontologies (FRBRoo, LRMoo, LRMer, FRBR Core, RDA, CIDOC CRM, Music Ontology, FaBiO, EDM, RiC-O).
    10→
    11→**Usage**:
    12→```bash
    13→python scripts/gen_mocho_diagram.py
    14→```
    15→
    16→**Outputs**: SVG and PDF files written to `output/`, named `mocho-<Class>-hierarchy-<timestamp>.{svg,pdf}`.
    17→
    18→**Dependencies**: `graphviz` Python package; Graphviz binaries must be installed (`brew install graphviz`).
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## scripts/extract_rda_properties.py
    23→
    24→**Purpose**: Parse RDA vocabulary XML files (OWL/RDF, from the RDA-Vocabularies release) and extract all datatype and object properties for every RDA entity namespace present in the Elements directory. Covers all 9 entity codes: Work (`w`), Expression (`e`), Manifestation (`m`), Item (`i`), Agent (`a`), Nomen (`n`), Place (`p`), Timespan (`t`), RDA Entity (`x`). Outputs a JSON array and a CSV table.
    25→
    26→**Usage**:
    27→```bash
    28→# All entities, default output (output/mapping_rda_properties.{json,csv}):
    29→python scripts/extract_rda_properties.py
    30→
    31→# Recursively scan all RDA-Vocabularies-* releases under ontology/:
    32→python scripts/extract_rda_properties.py --vocab-dir all
    33→
    34→# Specific entities only:
    35→python scripts/extract_rda_properties.py --entities w,e,m,i
    36→
    37→# Custom output prefix:
    38→python scripts/extract_rda_properties.py --output output/rda_wemi_properties
    39→```
    40→
    41→**Options**:
    42→| Flag | Default | Description |
    43→|------|---------|-------------|
    44→| `--vocab-dir` | `ontology/RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9/xml/Elements` | Elements directory, or `all` to recursively scan `--vocab-base` |
    45→| `--vocab-base` | `ontology/` | Base directory for the `all` scan |
    46→| `--output` / `-o` | `output/mapping_rda_properties` | Output file prefix; writes `PREFIX.json` and `PREFIX.csv` |
    47→| `--entities` | all available | Comma-separated entity codes to extract (e.g. `w,e,m,i`) |
    48→
    49→**Inputs**: Per entity code `{c}`: `<vocab-dir>/{c}.xml` (base labels/definitions), `<vocab-dir>/{c}/datatype.xml`, `<vocab-dir>/{c}/object.xml`. Only entities with all three files present are processed.
    50→
    51→**Outputs**:
    52→- `PREFIX.json` — JSON array; fields: `element_id`, `uri`, `entity`, `entity_code`, `label`, `prop_type`, `definition`, `vocab`
    53→- `PREFIX.csv` — CSV table; same fields
    54→- stdout — per-entity property counts and total summary. Always printed.
    55→
    56→**Dependencies**: Python standard library only (`xml.etree.ElementTree`, `argparse`, `pathlib`, `json`, `csv`).
    57→
    58→**Caveats**: English-language annotations (`xml:lang="en"`) are required for label and definition extraction; properties lacking them will have blank fields. `skos:scopeNote` is used as a fallback when `skos:definition` is absent.
    59→
    60→---
    61→
    62→## scripts/extract_rda_classes.py
    63→
    64→**Purpose**: Parse RDA vocabulary XML files (OWL/RDF, from the RDA-Vocabularies release) and extract all `owl:Class` declarations. Class definitions live in `c.xml` inside the Elements directory (14 classes as of RDA Vocabularies 5.4.9). Outputs a JSON array and a CSV table per run. Optionally prints the class hierarchy as an indented tree.
    65→
    66→**Usage**:
    67→```bash
    68→# Specific Elements directory (default):
    69→python scripts/extract_rda_classes.py
    70→
    71→# Recursively scan all RDA-Vocabularies-* releases under ontology/:
    72→python scripts/extract_rda_classes.py --vocab-dir all
    73→
    74→# Custom output prefix and tree display:
    75→python scripts/extract_rda_classes.py \
    76→    --vocab-dir ontology/RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9/xml/Elements \
    77→    --output output/mapping_rda_classes \
    78→    --tree
    79→```
    80→
    81→**Options**:
    82→| Flag | Default | Description |
    83→|------|---------|-------------|
    84→| `--vocab-dir` | `ontology/RDA-Vocabularies-5.4.9/xml/Elements` | Elements directory containing `c.xml`, or `all` to recursively scan `--vocab-base` |
    85→| `--vocab-base` | `ontology/` | Base directory for the `all` scan |
    86→| `--output` / `-o` | `output/mapping_rda_classes` | Output file prefix; writes `PREFIX.json` and `PREFIX.csv` |
    87→| `--tree` | off | Print the class hierarchy as an indented tree to stdout |
    88→
    89→**Inputs**: `<vocab-dir>/c.xml` — the RDA Classes OWL/RDF file. When `--vocab-dir all`, all `c.xml` files found recursively under `--vocab-base` that contain `owl:Class` declarations.
    90→
    91→**Outputs**:
    92→- `PREFIX.json` — JSON array; fields: `element_id`, `uri`, `label`, `subclass_of` (list), `status`, `definition`, `vocab`
    93→- `PREFIX.csv` — CSV table; same fields; `subclass_of` pipe-separated
    94→- stdout — summary of class counts per status; hierarchy tree if `--tree`
    95→
    96→**Dependencies**: Python standard library only (`xml.etree.ElementTree`, `argparse`, `pathlib`, `json`, `csv`).
    97→
    98→**Caveats**: Only `c.xml` contains `owl:Class` declarations in the RDA 5.x release; other entity files (`w.xml`, `e.xml`, etc.) hold property declarations only. English-language annotations (`xml:lang="en"`) are required for label and definition extraction.
    99→
   100→---
   101→
   102→## scripts/extract_fabio_wemi_properties.py
   103→
   104→**Purpose**: Extract all OWL properties from `fabio.owl` whose `rdfs:domain` or `rdfs:range` includes any WEMI-related class (`fabio:Work`, `fabio:Expression`, `fabio:Manifestation`, `fabio:Item`, or FRBR-based equivalents). Handles `owl:unionOf`/`owl:intersectionOf` class expressions and `owl:Restriction` value constraints when resolving domain/range.
   105→
   106→**Usage**:
   107→```bash
   108→python scripts/extract_fabio_wemi_properties.py
   109→```
   110→
   111→**Outputs**: Structured plain-text block printed to stdout, one property per block, fields: Property URI, rdf:type, rdfs:label, rdfs:comment/skos:definition, rdfs:domain, rdfs:range, rdfs:subPropertyOf.
   112→
   113→**Dependencies**: `rdflib` (`pip install rdflib`).
   114→
   115→**Caveats**: WEMI matching is by local name only (e.g. `Work`, `Expression`), which is sufficient for FaBiO/FRBR namespaces but could produce false positives if other vocabularies loaded into the same graph use the same local names for unrelated classes.
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## scripts/gen_all_import_terms.py
   120→
   121→**Purpose**: Scan all `output/mapping_*_to_*.csv` files and generate or update `mocho-odk/imports/<ontology>_terms.txt` files. Produces one file per source ontology prefix (`aco_terms.txt`, `crm_terms.txt`, `dct_terms.txt`, `fabio_terms.txt`, `frbr_terms.txt`, `mo_terms.txt`, `rico_terms.txt`) plus a merged `rda_terms.txt` for all RDA target IRIs. New IRIs are appended to existing files; nothing is overwritten or removed.
   122→
   123→**Usage**:
   124→```bash
   125→# Default: reads output/mapping_*_to_*.csv, writes to mocho-odk/imports/:
   126→python scripts/gen_all_import_terms.py
   127→
   128→# Custom paths:
   129→python scripts/gen_all_import_terms.py \
   130→    --output-dir output \
   131→    --out-dir mocho-odk/imports
   132→```
   133→
   134→**Options**:
   135→| Flag | Default | Description |
   136→|------|---------|-------------|
   137→| `--output-dir` | `output` | Directory containing `mapping_*_to_*.csv` files |
   138→| `--out-dir` | `mocho-odk/imports` | Directory to write `*_terms.txt` files |
   139→
   140→**Inputs**: All `output/mapping_*_to_*.csv` files. Three schemas are handled:
   141→- Standard (`source, rda, domain, range, match, remarks`): rows with `match` = direct or approximate only
   142→- Extended CRM (`adds source_uri, rda_uri`): same match filter; full URIs used directly
   143→- DCT (`dct_term, rda_iri, rda_label`): all rows included (file is pre-filtered during generation)
   144→- Vocabulary extraction files (`mapping_rda_*.csv`) have no `match` column and are silently skipped
   145→
   146→**Outputs**:
   147→- `<out-dir>/aco_terms.txt` — Audio Commons Ontology IRIs
   148→- `<out-dir>/crm_terms.txt` — CIDOC CRM class IRIs
   149→- `<out-dir>/dct_terms.txt` — Dublin Core Terms IRIs
   150→- `<out-dir>/fabio_terms.txt` — FaBiO IRIs
   151→- `<out-dir>/frbr_terms.txt` — FRBR Core IRIs
   152→- `<out-dir>/mo_terms.txt` — Music Ontology IRIs
   153→- `<out-dir>/rico_terms.txt` — RiC-O IRIs
   154→- `<out-dir>/rda_terms.txt` — RDA class and property IRIs (merged from all mapping files)
   155→
   156→**Dependencies**: Python standard library only (`csv`, `argparse`, `pathlib`, `re`).
   157→
   158→**Caveats**: RDA class IRIs use `http://` and property IRIs use `https://` (matching the RDA registry conventions used in existing mapping files). Adding a new source ontology requires extending the `SOURCE_PREFIX_TO_NS` and `PREFIX_TO_STEM` tables in the script.
   159→
   160→---
   161→
   162→## scripts/gen_vra_mapping.py
   163→
   164→**Purpose**: Generate the VRA Core 4.0 → RDA mapping tables (CSV and JSON). VRA Core's class hierarchy uses `schema:CreativeWork` and `foaf:Agent` — not FRBR — so the automated `gen_frbr_target_mapping.py` produces no output for it. This script provides the hand-curated alignment following the same static ROWS pattern as `gen_nonwemi_mapping.py`. Covers 7 VRA classes and 65 properties across creator roles, part-whole, derivative, subject, place, identification, and physical/technical groups; 48 approximate mappings and 24 no-match entries.
   165→
   166→**Usage**:
   167→```bash
   168→# Default output (output/mapping_vra_to_rda.{csv,json}):
   169→python scripts/gen_vra_mapping.py
   170→
   171→# Custom output prefix:
   172→python scripts/gen_vra_mapping.py --output output/mapping_vra_to_rda
   173→```
   174→
   175→**Options**:
   176→| Flag | Default | Description |
   177→|------|---------|-------------|
   178→| `--output` | `output/mapping_vra_to_rda` | Output file prefix; writes `PREFIX.csv` and `PREFIX.json` |
   179→
   180→**Inputs**: None — all mapping data is embedded in the `ROWS` list.
   181→
   182→**Outputs**:
   183→- `PREFIX.csv` — 72 rows; columns: `source, rda, domain, range, match, remarks`
   184→- `PREFIX.json` — same rows; `rda` field as a list (split on `;`)
   185→- stdout — row count, output paths, and match-level summary (direct / approximate / no match)
   186→
   187→**Dependencies**: Python standard library only (`argparse`, `csv`, `json`, `pathlib`, `collections.Counter`).
   188→
   189→**Caveats**: The mapping is hand-curated against VRA Core 4.0 (`ontology/vra-core-v4.0.owl`). VRA's 30+ creator-role properties all collapse to `rdaw:P10065` has creator agent of work; role granularity is lost. Event-mediated date properties (`wasCreated`, `wasProduced`, etc.) have no RDA structural equivalent and are marked no match. See `docs/vra-frbr-mapping.md`, `docs/vra-crm-mapping.md`, `docs/vra-fabio-mapping.md`, and `docs/vra-rda-mapping.md` for the full alignment analysis.
   190→
   191→



```

  "old_string": "## scripts/extract_rda_classes.py",
  "new_string": "## scripts/extract_ontology_terms.py\n\n**Purpose**: Load any ontology file in any rdflib-supported format (Turtle, RDF/XML, OWL, N3, N-Triples, JSON-LD, …), extract all declared classes and properties, and write a flat CSV and JSON table. Useful as a quick vocabulary inventory before running the full FRBR/RDA mapping pipeline.\n\n**Usage**:\n```bash\n# Default output prefix = output/<ontology-stem>:\npython scripts/extract_ontology_terms.py --ontology ontology/ddbedm_1.0.ttl\n\n# Custom output prefix:\npython scripts/extract_ontology_terms.py \\\n    --ontology ontology/RiC-O_1-1.rdf \\\n    --output output/rico_terms\n```\n\n**Options**:\n| Flag | Default | Description |\n|------|---------|-------------|\n| `--ontology` / `-i` | *(required)* | Path to the ontology file |\n| `--output` / `-o` | `output/<stem>` | Output file prefix; writes `PREFIX.csv` and `PREFIX.json` |\n\n**Outputs**:\n- `PREFIX.csv` / `PREFIX.json` — one row per term; columns: `type`, `label`, `iri`, `definition`\n  - `type`: `class` \\| `object property` \\| `datatype property` \\| `annotation property` \\| `property`\n  - `label`: `rdfs:label` (English preferred, any language as fallback, local IRI name if absent)\n  - `definition`: `skos:definition` if present; `rdfs:comment` otherwise; empty string if neither\n- stdout — total term count and per-type breakdown\n\n**Dependencies**: `rdflib` (`pip install rdflib`).\n\n**Caveats**: Only `URIRef` subjects are extracted; anonymous blank-node class expressions (e.g. `owl:Restriction`, `owl:unionOf`) are skipped. When a term has multiple type assertions (e.g. both `owl:Class` and `rdfs:Class`), the most specific type wins. All terms declared in the file are included regardless of namespace — re-declarations of external vocabulary terms will appear if typed in the file.\n\n---\n\n## scripts/extract_rda_classes.py"
}
